coredata.dc:title: A degradable polyimide aerogel with highly efficient solar-thermal-electric effect for oil absorption, deicing, and power generation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shiwei
Gao, Dongfang
Long, Lixia
Cui, Wenxuan
Zhu, Chaofeng
Wang, Xue
Li, Bing
Chen, Yu
Li, Yuanyue
Jiang, Chuanxing
coredata.dc:description: Photothermal materials are considered as promising materials because they can convert clean solar energy into thermal and electrical energy. However, developing degradable photothermal materials with highly efficient solar-thermal-electric energy conversion performance remains a huge challenge. Here, a superhydrophobic bio-polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel (S-BioPI/CQDs) is synthesized. S-BioPI/CQDs exhibits superhydrophobicity (WCA = 155°) and super lipophilicity (OCA = 0°). Remarkably, S-BioPI/CQDs shows good solar-thermal-electric energy conversion properties. The surface temperature of S-BioPI/CQDs can be up to 80 °C within 68 s under the solar light irradiation of 1 kW m−2. S-BioPI/CQDs has large crude oil adsorption capacity (up to 68.8 times as much as its own weight) and deicing under sunlight irradiation. Meanwhile, the output voltage can be up to 706 mV under the solar light irradiation of 5 kW m−2. S-BioPI/CQDs can resist the impact of harsh environments, such as high temperatures, dynamic ocean environments, and strong acid environment. More importantly, S-BioPI/CQDs can be degraded completely within only 8 min. This is the first time to achieve the degradation of PI aerogel. This study provides a new and effective method to prepare advanced photothermal materials with degradable performances for the efficient use of solar energy to solve the fossil fuel crisis and protect the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aerogel
Superhydrophobicity
Crude oil absorption
Solar-thermal-electric energy conversion
Degradation

Morphology and structure of BioPI and BioPI-1 %
Mechanical property of BioPI and BioPI-1 %
Stability of the aerogel in harsh environments

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental process for the preparation of S-BioPI/CQDs.
(a) FTIR, (b) XRD of BioPI and BioPI-1 %, SEM images of (c) BioPI, and (d) BioPI-1 %.
(a) Before and after 80 % strain for BioPI-1 %. (b) Compressive stress–strain curves of S-BioPI-1 % after 200 cycles at 50 % strain. (c) BET curves of BioPI and BioPI-1 %.

(a) Immersion process for S-BioPI-1 % in water. (b) Water contact angle and oil contact angle of S-BioPI-1 %. (c) Water droplet and oil droplet on S-BioPI-1 %. (d) Pictures of different liquids on S-BioPI-1 % surface. (e) Self-cleaning property of S-BioPI-1 %.

Images of S-BioPI-1 % absorbing (a) silicone oil and (b) carbon tetrachloride in a static state. (c) Images of S-BioPI-1 % absorbing silicone oil under strong stirring. (d) Continuous separation of oil and water.

(a) UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra of S-BioPI and S-BioPI-1 %. (b) Light-temperature change curve of S-BioPI and S-BioPI-1 % under 1 sunlight irradiation. (c) Light-temperature change curve of S-BioPI-1 % with different solar light power densities. (d) Deicing process of S-BioPI and S-BioPI-1 % under 1 kW m−2 light power density. (e) The crude oil adsorption process for S-BioPI-1 % without or with sunlight irradiation. The crude oil adsorption capacity of S-BioPI and S-BioPI/CQDs (f) without or (g) with sunlight irradiation. (h) Continuous oil absorption by BioPI-1 % without or with sun radiation. (i) Output voltage curves of S-BioPI-1 % as time changes. (j) Output voltage curves of S-BioPI-1 % with different solar light power densities. (k) Repeatability of output voltage when the solar light was turned on/off.

Crude oil absorption by S-BioPI-1 % in different environments. (a) Different pH values, (b) different oscillation times, and (c) different temperatures. (d) The crude oil adsorption capacity of S-BioPI-1 % after 9 cycles.

(a) The degradation process of S-BioPI-1 %. (b) FTIR of degraded S-BioPI-1 %. (c) GPC of S-BioPI-1 % and degraded S-BioPI-1 %. (d) Pictures, (e) FTIR, and (f) ultraviolet–visible spectrum of ethanolamine and R-Ethanolamine.
Comparisons of crude oil absorption capacity between our work and previous works.

head.ce:title: A degradable polyimide aerogel with highly efficient solar-thermal-electric effect for oil absorption, deicing, and power generation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photothermal materials are considered as promising materials because they can convert clean solar energy into thermal and electrical energy. However, developing degradable photothermal materials with highly efficient solar-thermal-electric energy conversion performance remains a huge challenge. Here, a superhydrophobic bio-polyimide/carbon quantum dots aerogel (S-BioPI/CQDs) is synthesized. S-BioPI/CQDs exhibits superhydrophobicity (WCA = 155°) and super lipophilicity (OCA = 0°). Remarkably, S-BioPI/CQDs shows good solar-thermal-electric energy conversion properties. The surface temperature of S-BioPI/CQDs can be up to 80 °C within 68 s under the solar light irradiation of 1 kW m−2. S-BioPI/CQDs has large crude oil adsorption capacity (up to 68.8 times as much as its own weight) and deicing under sunlight irradiation. Meanwhile, the output voltage can be up to 706 mV under the solar light irradiation of 5 kW m−2. S-BioPI/CQDs can resist the impact of harsh environments, such as high temperatures, dynamic ocean environments, and strong acid environment. More importantly, S-BioPI/CQDs can be degraded completely within only 8 min. This is the first time to achieve the degradation of PI aerogel. This study provides a new and effective method to prepare advanced photothermal materials with degradable performances for the efficient use of solar energy to solve the fossil fuel crisis and protect the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of social economy and technology, the demand for fossil fuels is increasing. However, environmental pollution caused by fossil fuels has become a global problem that urgently needs to be solved. Therefore, exploring and developing environmentally friendly and sustainable energy is crucial for the development of human society [1–3]. Among various renewable energy sources in nature, solar energy is the cleanest, most abundant, and easily accessible renewable energy source. Therefore, efficient utilization of solar energy resources is very important for alleviating global energy shortages and protecting the environment. Sustainable and environmental solar energy can be directly converted into heat through photothermal materials [4–6]. The operation is simple and the cost is low. The development of photothermal materials with high performance is the top priority for efficient utilization of solar energy [7–9]. The high absorption rate in the solar spectrum and high solar thermal efficiency for these materials are crucial [10,11]. The solar absorption rate of these materials is mainly related to their sensitivity to light, surface roughness, and specific surface area. Aerogel materials have become good photothermal devices due to their advantages with three-dimensional porous structures [12].

Polyimide (PI) is an organic polymer material with five-membered imine rings in the main chain. PI possesses an easily regulated structure with excellent thermal stability and mechanical properties [13–16]. PI aerogel has become a research hotspot due to its interconnected network structure, high porosity, large specific surface area, low density, and excellent chemical inertness [17–19]. PI aerogel can adapt to extreme environments such as high temperatures and strong acids. Therefore, PI aerogel has wide application prospects in the field of photothermal area. However, the disadvantages of PI aerogel, such as low photothermal conversion efficiency, large heat loss, and low light absorption rate, limit the improvement of photothermal performance and reduce the light energy utilization efficiency. Therefore, how to construct PI aerogel with efficient photothermal conversion is of great significance for the development of functional PI aerogel materials.

At present, some semiconductors have become effective photothermal conversion materials [20]. When the energy of photons on a semiconductor is greater than the semiconductor bandgap, the semiconductor will be excited to generate electron-hole pairs [21]. The excited electrons will eventually return to a low-energy state and energy is released concurrently. Carbon-based materials have the advantages of low cost, easy processing, and stable structure [22]. Carbon-based materials possess high light absorption capacity and have been recognized as one of the best photothermal materials [23–25]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are new carbon-based materials with spherical-like structures. These materials have the advantages of a simple preparation method, low toxicity, and environmental protection [26]. At the same time, CQDs have unique upconversion properties. They could absorb low-energy visible or near-infrared light and then emit high-energy ultraviolet light. Therefore, CQDs are expected to improve the light absorption efficiency of PI aerogel.

In addition, the raw materials for PI come from non-renewable petroleum resources, hindering its application [27–29]. Partial or comprehensive substitution of petroleum resources becomes an urgent demand and trend in the development of human society [30–32]. Biomass energy refers to the energy stored by green plants through photosynthesis in the natural environment [33–35]. Biomass is the only carbon energy that can replace petroleum resources. Biomass can effectively avoid environmental pollution and greenhouse effects caused by fossil energy combustion. How to convert biomass resources into non-toxic, efficient, and durable chemicals has become a hot research topic in recent years [36–38]. Bio-based platform compounds refer to products derived from biomass, which were able to synthesize high-value-added chemicals. 2, 5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA) is the only aromatic compound with a “rigid” structure among the bio-based platform compounds [39,40]. 2, 5-FDCA has become an important bridge between biomass resources and the petrochemical industry. Reasonable utilization of 2, 5-FDCA has great significance in alleviating environmental pollution caused by excessive fossil energy consumption. Unfortunately, the research on the preparation of PI aerogel using 2, 5-FDCA is still insufficient. With the increasingly widespread application of PI aerogels, the amount of PI waste products is increasing rapidly. Conventional methods including burning and landfilling cause serious pollution. The research on the degradability of PI aerogels can protect the environment, reduce energy consumption, and contribute to sustainable development. However, due to the stable molecular structure, the degradation of PI aerogel is very difficult [41,42]. Recently, PI was degraded under harsh conditions of high temperature and high pressure. High energy consumption and expensive processing costs are a huge challenge for practical industrial applications [43,44]. How to achieve PI aerogel degradation by a simple and efficient method becomes an important research subject.

Based on the research background, this work successfully prepared a photothermal PI aerogel with sustainable and degradable performance. The bio-based resources are used to replace petroleum-based raw materials. The novelty of this work was as follows: Firstly, compared with harsh conditions of high temperature and pressure, the degradation of aerogel was realized by a mild degradation method. The experimental time is short and the degradation effect is remarkable. Secondly, the photothermal effect of CQDs was explored further, and the photothermal conversion has been successfully applied to crude oil adsorption and deicing for PI aerogel. Thirdly, the power generation of PI aerogel was realized for the first time. This work provides a sustainable and degradable candidate material for the applications of solar-thermal-electric energy conversion.

Acetic anhydride, trimesoylchloride (TMC), citric acid, trimethylamine (Et3N), thionyl chloride (SOCl2), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 1,3,5-triaminophenoxybenzene (TAB), pyridine, OTC, di-(4-aminophenyl)ether (ODA), 2, 5-FDCA, N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), ethanol, and 3, 3′, 4, 4′-biphenyltetracarboxylic dianhydride (BPDA) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were used without any purification.

3.85 g of citric acid was added to DI water with ultrasound assistance. Then the solution was put into a polytetrafluoroethylene lined reactor. The temperature was increased to 200 °C and kept for 5 h. Followingly, a 1 kDa dialysis bag was used to purify it in deionized water for 24 h. After vacuum drying, black powder was obtained.

According to the literature, 2, 5-FDCA reacted with thionyl chloride to prepare 2, 5-furandicarbonyl dichloride [45]. Then, 30 mL of DMF, 24 mmol of Et3N, and 24 mmol ODA were added into a flask and kept stirring vigorously at room temperature. 15 min later, 20 mmol of 2, 5-furandicarbonyl dichloride and 20 mL of DMF were added and stirred for 24 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. Then, the solution was poured into water and the precipitate occurred. The precipitate was thoroughly washed with water and dried in an oven at 90 °C to obtain bio-based diamine (FDCA-OA).

ODA, DMF (80 mL), and FDCA-OA were added into a flask and stirred at room temperature. 30 min later, 14.6 mmol of BPDA was added in. After 10 min, TAB was put in and stirred for 24 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. Followingly, the solution was poured into water to get precipitate (PAA). 0.1 g of dried PAA and a certain amount of CQDs were dissolved in 20 mL of water. Then, 0.05 g of Et3N was added in and stirred for 6 h. The mixture was transferred to a refrigerator to prepare bio-based polyimide acid/CQDs aerogel (BioPAA/CQDs) by a freeze-drying method. BioPAA/CQDs was placed in a tube furnace at 300 °C for 2 h. Finally, the BioPI/CQDs was prepared.

The calculated amount (n wt %) of CQDs corresponding to BioPI was dissolved in the solution. During the freeze-drying process, only the water could be removed and the remaining CQDs could be fixed in the aerogel. The BioPI containing n wt % CQDs was denoted as BioPI-n %.

BioPI/CQDs was immersed in methyl trichlorosilane n-hexane solution (2 %, v/v) for 0.5 h. After extruding the solution, BioPI/CQDs was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 2 h to obtain S-BioPI/CQDs. The S-BioPI/CQDs containing n wt % of CQDs was denoted as S-BioPI-n %.

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were conducted according to a Nicolet NEXUS870 spectrometer within the range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction tests (XRD) were operated on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer, which was equipped with a nickel-filtered Cu Kα radiation and θ/2θ Bragg-Brentano geometry at a scanning speed of 5° min−1. The morphology was tested by scanning electron microscope images (SEM, ThermoFisher, QuattroC, USA). The samples were all sputtered with gold at 30 mA current for 30 s before observation. The photoluminescence spectra (PL) were tested on a fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4) using a laser (266 nm, 6 ns pulse width) as the excitation source. The specific surface area and pore volume were calculated using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method by Micromeritics ASAP 2020 using N2 desorption at 77.35 K. The absorption spectra were operated on an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2550, Japan). The cyclic compressing tests of aerogel were operated on a universal testing machine (SUNS, UTM4204, China). A CEL-HXF300-T3 Xe lamp was used as a simulated solar light source. The lamp was equipped with a standard AM1.5 G optical filter. A CEL-NP2000 optical power meter was used to measure the light intensity of the lamp. The temperatures were detected by a UX120-014 M Onset HOBO data collection system. A Keithley DMM7510 7½ Multimeter was used to measure the voltage outputs generated by thermoelectric devices. The molecular weights of the samples were tested by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) on a MINED-BLS separation system (Agilent, USA). DMF was used as the eluent with a flow rate of 1 mL min−1.

The aerogel was put in silicone oil for 10 min to finish its adsorption. The aerogel was weighed before and after the adsorption. The adsorption capacity (Q, g/g) of the aerogel was obtained using the following formula:
(1)
Q =
M t
-
M 0
/
M 0
where Mt (g) represents the aerogel weight after adsorption, M0 (g) represents the aerogel weight before adsorption, and Q (g/g) represents the adsorption capacity.
Silicone oil was used for the cyclic adsorption test. The aerogels were squeezed to release silicone oil.

A continuous separation device was designed to measure the oil/water separation property. There were three parts: a vacuum pump, an oil storage bottle, and BioPI/CQDs. The oil storage bottle was connected with BioPI/CQDs by the hose. Water was stained with methyl blue and silicone oil was stained with Oil Red O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crude oil adsorption under simulated sunlight irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The aerogel was placed on the surface of the crude oil. The solar simulator was used as the light source to illuminate the aerogel so that the aerogel could adsorb the crude oil with the aid of light heating. The aerogel was taken out after ten minutes. When there was no oil drop, its mass Mt was measured. The initial mass of the aerogel was M0, and then the adsorption capacity was measured using the formula (1).

Followingly, the crude oil in the aerogel was squeezed out, and the process was repeated. The change of adsorption capacity with the number of adsorption cycles was recorded and the cycle performance of aerogel under sunshine conditions was measured.

The aerogel was cut into small pieces. 0.5 g of aerogel was added to 20 mL of EA. The temperature was increased to 80 °C. EA could be recycled by vacuum distillation. The residual solution was the degraded product.

The preparation of S-BioPI/CQDs is displayed in Fig. 1 . Firstly, 2, 5-FDCA reacted with SOCl2 to obtain acyl chloride, which made an amidation reaction with ODA to synthesize FDCA-OA. Secondly, BPDA, ODA, TAB, and FDCA-OA were used to prepare PAA. At the same time, CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. Thirdly, BioPAA/CQDs aerogel was prepared by introducing PAA to CQDs solution using a freeze-drying method. Fourthly,BioPI/CQDs was obtained after thermal imidization. Finally, S-BioPI/CQDs was prepared by hydrophobic treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of BioPI and BioPI-1 %
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2
a shows the FTIR results of BioPI and BioPI-1 %. The peak at 1240 cm−1 is attributed to the COC vibration peak. The peak at 1373 cm−1 is assigned to the CN stretching vibration peak. The peak at 1500 cm−1 is assigned to benzene ring stretching vibration. The peaks at 1773 cm−1 and 1713 cm−1 are attributed to the CO stretching vibration. The peak at 1578 cm−1 is attributed to the CC furan stretching vibration peak. These peaks show the typical vibration peaks of BioPI. Notably, the peak intensity of 1773 cm−1, 1713 cm−1, and 1240 cm−1 of BioPI-1 % is stronger than that of BioPI. This is because the peaks of the COC and COOH groups in CQDs overlapped with those in BioPI. Fig. 2 b displays the XRD of BioPI and BioPI-1 %. A wide diffraction peak occurs on the XRD curves of BioPI and BioPI-1 %. The peak is attributed to the typical diffraction peak on PI. No sharp peak occurs on the XRD curves of BioPI and BioPI-1 %, displaying that the structures of BioPI and BioPI-1 % are amorphous. As shown in Fig. 2 b, the peak intensity at 24° in BioPI-1 % is stronger than that of BioPI. This is because the peaks at 24° of CQDs and BioPI-1 % are overlapped. CQDs are uniform nanodots with good dispersion (Fig. S1a). Their average lateral size is approximately 2 nm. According to XRD spectrum (Fig. S1b), the diffraction peak of CQDs is located at 2θ = 24°, confirming that the structures of CQDs are amorphous.

Fig. 2 c, d show SEM images of BioPI and BioPI-1 %. It can be seen that the images of BioPI and BioPI-1 % display similar structures and porous networks. As seen in Fig. 2 d, CQDs were fixed intimately to BioPI-1 %. This is attributed to π-π stacking interaction and strong quantum confinement of CQDs on the BioPI [46]. The porous structure is useful for the adsorption property of bio-based aerogel. Thus, the active sites on the BioPI can contact adsorbed molecules more easily.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical property of BioPI and BioPI-1 %
ce:section.ce:para: Structural stability and elasticity are very important for adsorbing materials to achieve recycling and reuse. According to Fig. 3 a, when a certain pressure was applied perpendicular to S-BioPI-1 %, S-BioPI-1 % was compressed by about 80 %. After releasing the pressure, the aerogel quickly returned to its original size, indicating that the PI aerogel has good compressibility and elasticity. According to Fig. 3b, the tensile strength of BioPI-1 % was 14.1 kPa. After 200 cycles of strain at 50 %, tensile strength was 12.7 kPa (remained at 90 %), confirming that S-BioPI-1 % has good resilience. The excellent mechanical properties of S-BioPI-1 % are beneficial for its reutilization in the field of oil/water separation. Fig. 3c shows BET results of BioPI and BioPI-1 %. According to Fig. 3c, hysteresis loops were found on the plots of BioPI and BioPI-1 %, confirming that the structures of aerogels are mesoporous. The mesoporous structure is conducive to mass transfer and prohibits the recombination of photogenerated carriers. It can be seen that the hysteresis loop of BioPI-1 % is larger and moves up. The phenomenon shows that the pore volume of BioPI-1 % is larger than that of BioPI. The surface area of BioPI-1 % is 274 m2/g, which is higher than 9 m2/g of PI powder [47]. The large surface area is helpful for adsorption.
To improve the oil/water separation ability of aerogel and expand its application in the field of oil/water separation, aerogel is modified through hydrophobicprocessing to obtain S-BioPI-1 %.

The porous structures of aerogel endow materials with ultra-low density. The density of S-BioPI-1 % is 22.9 mg/mL. S-BioPI-1 % was put into a beaker with water and could float on the water surface as shown in Fig. 4
a. Water can not penetrate the aerogel, thus the aerogel can float on the water surface for a long time. Even if it is immersed in water under the action of external force, it can quickly float on water surface once the external force is removed. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the water contact angle of S-BioPI-1 % is 155°, confirming that S-BioPI-1 % possesses superhydrophobic properties. The oil contact angle of S-BioPI-1 % is 0°, exhibiting super lipophilicity. When the silicone oil (dyed red) and the water (dyed yellow) are dropped on the surface of S-BioPI-1 %, the silicone oil is quickly adsorbed by S-BioPI-1 % and spread on its surface. In contrast, the yellow water droplet is spherical and stays on the surface of S-BioPI-1 %. The process of oil dropping into the aerogel was recorded in Fig. 4 c, and it is found that the oil can be completely adsorbed within 0.9 s. It should be noted that different liquid drops (vinegar, milk, tea, coffee, and soy sauce) were put on the aerogel surface as shown in Fig. 4 d. It is observed all these liquid drops are spherical on the surface of PI aerogel and have good stability. The experiment shows that S-BioPI-1 % has superhydrophobicity and super lipophilicity. Therefore, it is expected that S-BioPI-1 % possesses self-cleaning performance and can selectively adsorb oil in an oil/water mixture for oil/water separation.

Self-cleaning materials can keep surfaces clean under natural conditions, save labor, and have great practicality in daily life. Analogous to the hydrophobic self-cleaning of lotus leaves, the hydrophobic surface of lotus leaves has special wettability. When small water droplets deposit on the surface, they form spherical droplets due to the lower surface energy. Thus, the contact area between the water droplets and the substrate surface is reduced. When the water droplets contact pollutant particles, pollutant particles are carried away by the rolling of the water droplets. To explore the self-cleaning effect of the aerogel, S-BioPI-1 % was slightly tilted so that the water droplets could fall freely under their gravity. The activated carbon was put on the aerogel surface and deionized water was added dropwise as shown in Fig. 4 e. It is observed that the water droplets fall behind and the activated carbon on the surface is taken away by the flowing water droplets soon. The experiment confirms that S-BioPI-1 % has self-cleaning property and can keep its surface from dust pollution. Under natural conditions, the dust on the surface of S-BioPI-1 % can fall off automatically with the help of rain to reduce cleaning costs.

To investigate the selective adsorption of oil in an oil/water mixture by S-BioPI-1 %, silicone oil (dyed red) as light oil or carbon tetrachloride (dyed red) as heavy oil was mixed with water respectively. As displayed in Fig. 5
a, silicone oil was covered on the water surface. When S-BioPI-1 % was put into the mixture, the aerogel quickly adsorbed silicone oil. At the same time, S-BioPI-1 % could continue to float on the surface after the adsorption of silicone oil. As shown in Fig. 5 b, carbon tetrachloride sank to the bottom. When S-BioPI-1 % was immersed underwater, its hydrophobicity prevented water from penetrating the aerogel. Once S-BioPI-1 % contacts carbon tetrachloride, its super lipophilicity makes carbon tetrachloride quickly penetrate the aerogel. 5 s later, the adsorption was finished, and there was no residual carbon tetrachloride in the water (Fig. 5 b).Therefore, S-BioPI-1 % can realize selective oil adsorption in the oil/water mixture.

The oil slick above is in a static state, while oil slick in the sea or industrial wastewater is often flowing. Therefore, it is very important to study the oil adsorption ability of the aerogel in a complex environment. Here, an experiment is designed to simulate the dynamic separation process of oil/water mixtures using aerogel materials (Fig. 5 c). Silicone oil was on the static water surface. Under strong stirring, the mixture turned red. When S-BioPI-1 % was put in, the mixture quickly became colorless and all silicone oil was adsorbed, indicating that the aerogel maintains excellent oil adsorption capacity in complex environments.

In the above adsorption process, S-BioPI-1 % hardly adsorbs water. This is because S-BioPI-1 % has a large number of hydrophobic groups, and the pores in the aerogel are replaced by solvents that are not miscible with water. The superhydrophobicity and super lipophilicity enabled S-BioPI-1 % to have a rapid oil permeation flux, avoiding the direct contact between water and S-BioPI-1 % in the oil/water separation process. Thus the separation performance of S-BioPI-1 % is greatly enhanced, revealing its potential application in the field of oil/water separation.

The adsorption capacity of S-BioPI-1 % for silicone oil is 98.06 times its weight. The reusability of oil adsorption materials is of great significance for improving oil adsorption efficiency and reducing the cost of oil adsorption materials. The mechanical extrusion method is commonly used for recycling oil adsorption materials. The extrusion method involves manually/mechanically squeezing elastic oil adsorption materials. This method causes the oil adsorbed inside the aerogel to overflow and restores its oil adsorption capacity. Although extrusion cannot completely extrude the adsorbed oil and restore oil adsorption capacity, its fast and simple characteristics make this method a preferred method for oil adsorption materials. PI aerogel is a porous material with compression resilience. Therefore, adsorbed oil can be squeezed out by mechanical extrusion. The recovered oil can be processed and reused, greatly protecting resources. After 200 consecutive cycles of oil adsorption and drainage experiments, the oil adsorption capacity of S-BioPI-1 % remains at 95 %, showing good reusability. The good reusability of S-BioPI-1 % is related to its high elastic property and superior compression fatigue resistance.

It can be seen from the above result that the superhydrophobicity/super lipophilicity of aerogel realizes the selective adsorption of oil in the oil/water mixture, which is beneficial to the oil/water separation efficiency. For the adsorption and collection of floating oil on large areas of seawater, oil adsorption materials are limited due to their limited adsorption capacities. Continuous oil adsorption can effectively solve this problem with the help of a vacuum pump. As shown in Fig. 5 d, silicone oil (dyed red) and water (dyed blue) were separated continuously by a vacuum pump. S-BioPI-1 % is inserted into one end of a pipe, which is then put in the oil/water mixture. The other end of the pipe is connected to the oil storage bottle. When the aerogel is immersed in the oil/water mixture, the oil can be adsorbed quickly and the porous aerogel is filled with oil soon, resulting in the blockage of the air channel inside the aerogel. Therefore, under the action of the vacuum pump, the oil adsorbed by the aerogel can be constantly sucked into the oil storage bottle to achieve continuous oil/water separation. When all the oil in the mixture is adsorbed, the air channel inside the aerogel is reopened under the effect of the vacuum, ensuring that water can not be sucked into the aerogel. The oil adsorption capacity of porous aerogel is no longer limited, so it can be used for the continuous collection of oil on large bodies of water.

UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra of S-BioPI and S-BioPI-1 % were shown in Fig. 6
a. According to Fig. 6 a, the UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra intensity of S-BioPI-1 % is higher than that of S-BioPI. This phenomenon indicates the light absorption property of S-BioPI-1 % is enhanced, showing that CQDs increase the visible light absorption capacity. This is because CQDs absorb low-energy light and emit high-energy light due to their special upconversion. Thus, S-BioPI/CQDs can absorb more visible light. Intrinsic characteristics of polymer semiconductors contribute to the strong electron coupling of S-BioPI/CQDs. The charge can move easily in a simple polymer chain. So the charge transfer among different polymer chains is the key to the charge transfer rate [48]. CQDs become the intermediary for charge transfer among different polymer chains.

Temperature is an important parameter to evaluate the photothermal conversion efficiency of the aerogel. Fig. 6 b shows the surface temperature curves of the aerogels under 1 kW m−2 light power density. The surface temperature of S-BioPI-1 % rapidly increases to 80.3 °C from room temperature and reaches equilibrium, while the surface temperature of S-BioPI is only 58.1 °C after equilibrium. The result further confirms that CQDs improve the light absorption intensity. CQDs loaded on the aerogel framework convert sunlight into heat, which is transferred to the surrounding areas through thermal radiation. Meanwhile, the porous structure ensures that the CQDs inside the aerogel can also absorb the incident sunlight, which is refracted and reflected in the network structure of the aerogel. In addition, free electrons inside the aerogels are excited from π-orbitals to π* orbitals under illumination. When the photon energy of the light matches the energy required for electronic transition, the excited electrons will transition from the ground state orbit to a higher energy orbital and ultimately return to the ground state, releasing thermal energy. After turning off the light source, the surface temperature of S-BioPI-1 % rapidly decreases. Fig. 6 c shows the temperature curves under different sunlight irradiations. The temperature curves in Fig. 6 c display that the heating rate accelerates and the temperature value gets higher with the increase of light intensity. When the light power density is 1 kW m−2, the surface temperature of S-BioPI-1 % can reach up to 80 °C within 68 s.

Under 1 kW m−2 light conditions, it takes 370 s for ice on S-BioPI to be melted. In contrast, only 250 s are needed for ice on S-BioPI-1 % to be melted (Fig. 6 d). It shows that S-BioPI-1 % has good photothermal conversion performance, which can quickly absorb sunlight, convert sunlight into heat, and accelerate the melting of ice. When crude oil was put on NaCl solution at room temperature, the crude oil floated on the solution surface as shown in Fig. 6 e. The crude oil can not be adsorbed due to its poor fluidity and high viscosity. When the solar simulator is turned on, S-BioPI-1 % can quickly adsorb the crude oil. This is because S-BioPI-1 % can convert the sunlight into heat energy, thereby reducing the viscosity of the crude oil and promoting the adsorption of crude oil.

Fig. 6 f shows the adsorption capacity of samples for crude oil with and without sunlight at room temperature. It can be seen that there is no significant difference in the crude oil adsorption capacity between S-BioPI and S-BioPI/CQDs without sunlight, and their adsorption capacity for crude oil can be ignored. Fig. 6 g shows the adsorption capacity of each sample when the light power density is 1 kW m−2. Compared with S-BioPI, S-BioPI/CQDs could capture more sunlight and convert it into heat energy. It was found that S-BioPI-1 % possessed the highest oil capacity. This is because when the amount of CQDs is higher than 1 wt %, the dispersion is poor. CQDs are easy to aggregate into large particles. Micropores and nanopores are easy to block, affecting the oil absorption performance. When the content is 0.5 %, the photothermal effect is weak, resulting in low oil absorption capacity. When the adsorption time reached 10 min, the crude oil adsorption capacity of S-BioPI-1 % was up to 68.8 g/g, significantly higher than S-BioPI. The crude oil adsorption capacity is larger than other reported three-dimensional porous adsorbents (Table 1 ). In addition, it can be seen that the adsorption capacity of the samples for crude oil decreases with increasing content of CQDs. The reason is that more CQDs could accumulate into larger particles, resulting in lower adsorption capacity. Therefore, an appropriate amount of CQDs loading is necessary for aerogels to have excellent photothermal conversion ability and large adsorption capacity.

It is an effective and feasible method to adsorb crude oil using porous aerogels. Here, the crude oil/water separation performance of aerogel materials assisted by a vacuum pump is studied as shown in Fig. 6 h. When the sunlight simulator is turned off, it is difficult for crude oil to be pumped into the conical flask. After 60 min, there is still no crude oil in the empty conical flask, showing poor adsorption property. When the sunlight simulator is turned on, the crude oil can be adsorbed into the conical flask within 10 min. The main reason for the above phenomenon is that the S-BioPI-1 % directly converts the absorbed sunlight into heat energy. The temperature of the material rapidly increases and heat energy is transferred to contact crude oil, resulting in its viscosity decline. Thus the fluidity and wettability of crude oil increase, and it is easier for crude oil to enter the pores of the aerogels. Finally, the fluidity and diffusion rate of crude oil into the porous aerogels are significantly improved.

Solar thermoelectric generators can utilize the temperature difference to achieve solar thermoelectric energy conversion based on the Seebeck effect. The generators consist of a solar simulator, thermoelectric devices, and photothermal materials. The cold side was connected to a radiator (Fig. S2). Under the light power density of 5 kW m−2, the thermoelectric device covered by S-BioPI-1 % can generate a maximum voltage of nearly 706 mV (Fig. 6 i). Owing to the excellent solar thermal energy conversion performance, S-BioPI-1 % can absorb sunlight and quickly convert the sunlight into heat. It is worth noting that after stopping the exposure to sunlight, the output voltage gradually disappears. Subsequently, as the optical power density increases, the generated voltage gradually increases (Fig. 6 j). After several switching cycles, the generated output voltage remains stable, confirming that S-BioPI-1 % has stable solar-thermal-electric energy conversion performance (Fig. 6 k).

Based on the results above, a mechanism of solar-thermal-electric effect for S-BioPI/CQDs was proposed as follows (Fig. S3): Firstly, CQDs increased the visible light absorption capacity. Secondly, CQDs absorbed low-energy light and emitted high-energy light due to their special upconversion. Thirdly, CQDs converted sunlight into heat, which is transferred to the surrounding areas through thermal radiation. A thermoelectric generator was made of a solar simulator, thermoelectric devices, a radiator (cold side), and BioPI/CQDs (hot side). The generator can utilize the temperature difference to achieve power generation based on the Seebeck effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the aerogel in harsh environments
ce:section.ce:para:
Because the leakage of crude oil often occurs in harsh environments, the aerogels need to have excellent stability in harsh environments. Thus, the stability of the aerogels in harsh environments was measured. Firstly, S-BioPI-1 % is soaked in pH = 1, 5, 7, 9, or 0.1 M NaCl for 12 h, respectively. The adsorption capacity of crude oil is about 65.2, 65.3, 65.7, 65.3, and 67.1 g/g as shown in Fig. 7
a. This result confirms S-BioPI-1 % has resistance to strong acid, weak alkalinity, and salt solution. The magnetic stirrer is used to agitate 0.1 M NaCl violently to simulate the sea oscillation environment. S-BioPI-1 % is put in the oscillation solution for 48 h. The adsorption capacity is 67.7 g/g, showing that S-BioPI-1 % can still maintain a stable adsorption capacity in the turbulent environment as shown in Fig. 7 b. S-BioPI-1 % is put on the condition of −80 °C or 300 °C for 12 h, respectively. The adsorption capacity of crude oil is 67.9 g/g and 68.2 g/g, respectively, indicating that it has excellent high and low temperature resistance as shown in Fig. 7 c.

The crude oil in the aerogel is recycled by simple extrusion. S-BioPI-1 % is subjected to the adsorption test under 1 kW m−2 light powder density. The crude oil is recovered by manual extrusion. Then, S-BioPI-1 % is used for adsorption recovery again. Fig. 7 d showed that the adsorption capacity of S-BioPI-1 % can still be over 50.0g/g after 9 cycles. Excellent environmental adaptability endows S-BioPI-1 % with the ability to deal with oil spill accidents in harsh environments. Although aerogel materials at this stage are facing the challenges of high cost and low automation efficiency, their excellent performance makes aerogels have good application prospects in practical application.

Since the bond energy of CN is weak to be broken easily, ethanolamine is selected to break the bond in the imide ring of PI. S-BioPI-1 % is added to ethanolamine, and the result shows that it can completely dissolve within 8 min at 80 °C (Fig. 8
a). The result proves that S-BioPI-1 % has good chemical degradability to the specific reagent. This is of great significance for the treatment of plastic waste. The chemical structure of PI aerogel after degradation was tested by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 8 b). Comparing with PI aerogel before degradation (Fig. 2 a), the diffraction peak 1373 cm−1 of CN disappears, indicating the imine bond is broken. At the same time, a new peak of CH2 appears in 2933 cm−1, indicating that ethanolamine disrupts the imine ring and grafts onto it [57]. The molecular weights of the samples before and after degradation were measured by GPC as shown in Fig. 8 c. The weight average molecular weight and number average molecular weight of S-BioPI-1 % before degradation are 2.3 × 105 g mol−1 and 7.4 × 104 g mol−1. After degradation, the weight average molecular weight and number average molecular weight decrease to 1.6 × 104 g mol−1 and 8.4 × 103 g mol−1. Ethanolamine is recovered through vacuum distillation (Fig. 8 d).

The recovered ethanolamine (R-Ethanolamine) was measured by FTIR and ultraviolet–visible spectrum. Fig. 8 e shows the FTIR results of ethanolamine and R-Ethanolamine. Their FTIR curves are similar. The peak at 2947 cm−1 belongs to the CH2 stretching vibration. The peak at 1595 cm−1 is attributed to NH bending vibration. The peak at 1070 cm−1 is assigned to CO stretching vibration. The peak at 865 cm−1 is ascribed to CN bending vibration. According to the ultraviolet–visible spectrum result as shown in Fig. 8 f, ethanolamine and R-Ethanolamine showed the same ultraviolet–visible spectrum. These results confirm that R-Ethanolamine has high purity, indicating that ethanolamine can be recycled multiple times.

In summary, a degradable PI aerogel was fabricated and prepared successfully by a freeze-drying method for the first time. S-BioPI/CQDs possessed high elasticity and fatigue resistance. S-BioPI/CQDs displayed superhydrophobicity and excellent self-cleaning performance. S-BioPI/CQDs exhibited good solar-thermal-electric energy conversion properties, achieving crude oil adsorption, deicing, and electric power generation under solar light irradiation. S-BioPI/CQDs was degraded completely in ethanolamine within only 8 min to realize the degradation of PI aerogel for the first time. The study supplies theoretical guidance for preparing the advanced photothermal aerogel and shows attractive prospect for sustainable development in the field of photothermal materials.

Shiwei Chen: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Dongfang Gao: Conceptualization. Lixia Long: Formal analysis. Wenxuan Cui: Investigation. Chaofeng Zhu: Writing – review & editing. Xue Wang: Visualization. Bing Li: Project administration. Yu Chen: Software. Yuanyue Li: Software. Chuanxing Jiang: Software.

This work is jointly supported by Key R&D Program of Shandong Province, China (No. 2020CXGC010506), Jinan University and Institute Innovation Team Project of Jinan Science and Technology Bureau, China (No. 202228050), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176104), Major Science and Technology Projects in Inner Mongolia (No. 2021ZD0007), China and International Cooperation Project of the Pilot Project of Integration of Science, Education and Production of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), China (No. 2022GH020).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.12.024.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
